words: SAM SCHRAGER: I wondered why there were so many women here.
IONE ADAIR: Why the women went out there. Well, in the first place, the locater
out that part of the country had a wife that wanted a timber claim.
A sister that wanted a timber claim; two sisters that wanted timber
claims. And there was a couple of women here in town that wanted tim
ber claims that we all knew. So, when the locater had located this
particular batch of timber out in what was called the Forty-Nine Mea
dow country, and he took this bunch out to show them the country and
the timber and if they wanted to locate, then we could all locate there
with him, a certain distance of each other. Practically a little colony
by themselves. So that's the way we all happened to go out there. I
joined the fcunch with Mrs. Torson and Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Taylor and
Winifred Calkins and Kit Calkins and who else? Oh, following us when
we located out there was Erwin Hanson and Art Larson went out with him
just to be on the homestead with him so he'd have company. And they
were on one side and they were four miles and a half off on one side
and Mrs. Torson was in three-quarters of a mile from Mrs. Durham and
my homestead was about four miles over on the other side and Mrs.
Taylor's was over that way. And so we all got together just as a group
and would take our trips out together. And it was a saving on packers
and so forth when we all went together.

speaker: SS
words: Were most of these ladies as self-reliant as you were?

speaker: IA
words: I didn't quite hear you.

speaker: SS
words: It seemed like to be a homesteader, it took a lot of self-reliance,

speaker: SS
words: you know, you had to depend on yourself and your own resources.

speaker: IA
words: You had to depend upon yourself. But the others, of course, surrounding

speaker: IA
words: you that were homesteaders- had took up homesteacfe, all helped if you

speaker: IA
words: needed any help or anything like that. And Mrs. Durham and I planned winter

speaker: IA
words: together. I was four miles and a half from her place and

speaker: IA
words: so we decided that she and her daughter and I would live together that

speaker: IA
words: winter and I would go back and forth to my place as I wished. And it

speaker: IA
words: mostly,

speaker: IA
words: was all snowshoeing of course,.and that's the way we traveled. Then my

speaker: IA
words: father was afraid that something might happen and that we'd have no

speaker: IA
words: means of getting word out to the outside of an emergency. So he and

speaker: IA
words: Mrs. Durham and I decided that we'd hire a man to stay with us that

speaker: IA
words: winter. And so my father made the arrangements with a man by the name

speaker: IA
words: Carter. And Bill Carter said that he would trap that winter and would

speaker: IA
words: try to make up for any additional expenses that he would do- or needed,

speaker: IA
words: and that he would go out once a month anyway to get the mail and bring

speaker: IA
words: it to us, and the rest of the time he would spend trapping in that part

speaker: IA
words: of the country.

speaker: SS
words: Was he staying at the same cabin that you and-

speaker: IA
words: Mrs. Durham and her daughter and I- we were the three that were And Mrs. Torson,

speaker: IA
words: who was a here in town, had a cabin, oh,

speaker: IA
words: about three-quarters of a mile from out place. But she only spent the

speaker: IA
words: summers out there, her sister ran the business here in town and she

speaker: IA
words: went out and spent it on the homestead while the weather was good.

speaker: SS
words: I was just wondering; when he was there, did he have to sleep in the

speaker: SS
words: barn? No Barn.

speaker: SS
words: Did he have to sleep in the house because there was no barn there? He

speaker: SS
words: could sleep in the house too, then?

speaker: IA
words: He slept in the house with us; yes. We had, I don't know whether you

speaker: IA
words: know bear grass or not, but it's a very wiry grass and holds up when

speaker: IA
words: you made your mattress of it, and we made a full siae mattress. He

speaker: IA
words: was over six feet tall. (Chuckles) We made a full sized mattress for

speaker: IA
words: him of : and each day we would roll it up, put a blanket over it and

speaker: IA
words: could be used as a seat in case that you wanted extra accommodations.

speaker: SS
words: Just a mat of bear grass?

speaker: IA
words: Uh-huh. Just a mattress of bear grass. We made the mattress out of

speaker: IA
words: gunny sacks that we had and then cut the bear grass and made the mat

speaker: IA
words: tress for him. And that was his bedroom. And we had a bedroom across

speaker: IA
words: the end of the cabin, and I had a single bed and Mrs. Durham and her

speaker: IA
words: daughter had a double bed. And during the daytime we had fixed them

speaker: IA
words: so they would- the double bed would fold up in the wall and out of the

speaker: IA
words: way and the single bed used as a seat in the daytime.

speaker: IA
words: BERNADINE ADAIR CORNELISON: Didn't you have an attic in that place, a second story?

speaker: IA
words: Yes, we had an attic, and that's where we kept the flour and sugar and

speaker: IA
words: things that would- that the moisture might affect. We had an outdoor

speaker: IA
words: cellar and we kept the canned goods in this outdoor cellar, but the

speaker: IA
words: cupboard was the attic. We kept the flour and the sugar because that

speaker: IA
words: would be warm from the heat in the cabin, and it wouldn't get solid.

speaker: IA
words: Carter, one day, got everything all ready to go out the mail and

speaker: IA
words: left early in the morning for the we called it twenty-eight miles

speaker: IA
words: out to Avery by trail, and he didn't go back into Clarkia, because

speaker: IA
words: that was a longer route, it took forty-five miles to get in that way.

speaker: IA
words: So, he went out the short way to Avery, and the mail was all sent to

speaker: IA
words: Avery, and he would go out and get it. Well, he got everything all

speaker: IA
words: ready and left early and after he left Mrs. Durham decided that she

speaker: IA
words: had to wash, and the extra soap and stuff that she needed was up in

speaker: IA
words: the attic. And the only way into the attic was by ladder and a trap

speaker: IA
words: door. So, we brought in the ladder and opened the trapdoor and Mrs.

speaker: IA
words: Durham went up to the attic to get the flour or whatever it was that

speaker: IA
words: she needed and about the time she got up there the ice on the legs

speaker: IA
words: of the ladder broke off and the ladder went down. Mrs. Durham went

speaker: IA
words: with it, and as she went down she hit the end of her spine on the lad

speaker: IA
words: der and dropped off in a faint. Well Myrtle and I didn't know what to

speaker: IA
words: do, so we just grabbed up bottle of camphor that was there and in

speaker: IA
words: tending to put it up under her nose or where she'd breathe it, and

speaker: IA
words: Myrtle miscalculated and put it on her eye- on her forehead that ran

speaker: IA
words: down into her eye. Well, that brought Mrs. Durham to in one grand

speaker: IA
words: rush! We grabbed her while she was in that condition and put her over

speaker: IA
words: on my bed and she laid there all afternoon, just moaning, "Oh, my tailbone,

speaker: IA
words: oh, my tailbone." And nearly drove us crazy because we didn't

speaker: IA
words: know what to do about it until Carter got back anyway. We couldn't

speaker: IA
words: send word out; we couldn't do anything. And we neither one of us were

speaker: IA
words: going off and leaving the other one alone with the mother on a twentyeight

speaker: IA
words: mile trp out to town.

speaker: IA
words: So we just endured moaning and tried to

speaker: IA
words: keep her as comfortable as possible. Well, when Carter got back then

speaker: IA
words: he could see that Mrs. Durham was in a very bad condition and would

speaker: IA
words: have to be taken out to town. And so, we waited for about a week un

speaker: IA
words: til she got to feeling a little better and then Carter took her and

speaker: IA
words: started off to Avery. That was a twenty-eight mile trip for a woman

speaker: IA
words: with her tailbone injured and on snowshoes! So she got along very ni

speaker: IA
words: cely until about the last mile and a half or two miles of the trip and

speaker: IA
words: then she broke down. She just couldn't go any farther, so Carter

speaker: IA
words: practically carried her on to Avery and then they got transpor

speaker: IA
words: tation from Avery on the train and brought her down to Teko and on

speaker: IA
words: into Moscow. So we had her in the hospital for quite a little while

speaker: IA
words: here after that til she began to get better. So Myrtle and I were

speaker: IA
words: then left alone with just Carter as companion. And so we got along

speaker: IA
words: very nicely. But the next time Carter went out to Avery to see how

speaker: IA
words: Mrs. Durham was getting along and it left Myrtle and I alone, and we

speaker: IA
words: were sitting eating our breakfast one morning and the cabin door opened

speaker: IA
words: and in walked this grizzly, funny looking, little, old fellow and he

speaker: IA
words: said, "Oh, you're here." And we said, "Yes, who are yo u?" He said,

speaker: IA
words: "I'm Dynamite." And I said, "You are?" He said, "Yes, they all call

speaker: IA
words: me Dynamite." He said, "I'm working on the railroad"and on the tunnel

speaker: IA
words: and I do the drilling for the holes and putting the dynamite in, and

speaker: IA
words: that's where I got my name." I said, "You do have another name, don't

speaker: IA
words: you?" He said, "Yes, I'm Joel/chandler Harris." (Chuckles) And, he

speaker: IA
words: said, "My mother thought the name of Joel Chandler sounds very nice.

speaker: IA
words: She'd been reading about him, and so she called me Joel Chandler Harris."

speaker: IA
words: Well he came in and he stayed- he said, "I'll stay here until your man

speaker: IA
words: gets back." So he stayed; we couldn't say, "Get out!" We had no means

speaker: IA
words: of putting him out or anything and so we said, "Alright." So he took

speaker: IA
words: the bed- unrolled the bed that night and then that was his bed til Car

speaker: IA
words: ter got back. Well, when Carter got back then Dynamite left and went

speaker: IA
words: over to Larson and Hanson's cabin and he spent the greater part of the

speaker: IA
words: winter at the Larson and Hanson cabin. He was a funny looking little

speaker: IA
words: fellow. Whiskers all over.

speaker: SS
words: Did he stay there?

speaker: IA
words: He said, "ive been living in your cabin while you were gone." (Laughs)

speaker: IA
words: "We knew somebody had been there but we had no idea who nor what." He

speaker: IA
words: said,"I was in there. But when your man gets back I'll go over to the

speaker: IA
words: boys." And that's what he did.

speaker: SS
words: Was he staying there to protect you? Was that the idea?

speaker: IA
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Was he staying there to protect you? Was that his thinking?

speaker: IA
words: We don't know. We don't know what his idea was, he was just looking

speaker: IA
words: a place to land,,, and he just landed. And so we did a lot of blanket

speaker: IA
words: washing and all when- after he left.(Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: You know, he stayed at Elsie place, too. Because I know Carol

speaker: SS
words: Brink once mentioned that to me. She mentioned this guy Dynamite.

speaker: IA
words: She spoke about Dynamite, too, did she?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, she did.

speaker: IA
words: Well, Elsie was across on what they called another branch of the little

speaker: IA
words: Norseman, called the Floodwood. It went south and east. It went south

speaker: IA
words: and east and the Potlatch went the other way, and we were on the other

speaker: IA
words: side of the ridge from Elsie. She was quite a character. You should

speaker: IA
words: have known her. (Chuckles)

speaker: BC
words: She was delightful.

speaker: IA
words: She certainly was. One night we were going on the trip, my fatherafter

speaker: IA
words: I'd been out there so long, the government decided I had to come

speaker: IA
words: in to a trail at Wallace. And in order to get to the trial at Wallace

speaker: IA
words: I had to be notified to get out there, and there was no one in this

speaker: IA
words: part of the country that knew where I was or location or anything, and

speaker: IA
words: so my father undertook to come out and notify me; which he did. And

speaker: IA
words: he left word that we would need a packer to bring us out, and he wal

speaker: IA
words: ked out that twenty-five miles from Avery, or twenty-eight miles, and

speaker: IA
words: told me that I had to come in for the trip, And so they got

speaker: IA
words: the packer out and the packer, Ira Mc Peek, lived at Clarkia, and

speaker: IA
words: he had been packing things with the Forest so that he knew where we

speaker: IA
words: were and how to get to us and so forth. And he brought the horses out

speaker: IA
words: for us. And he brought a horse out for me to ride that an Indian had

speaker: IA
words: owned, and he lost the race for the Indian and the Indian was so mad

speaker: IA
words: that he cut the horse's tailfoff and his ears right close to his head.

speaker: IA
words: Well, the day that we started out with the packtrain after my father

speaker: IA
words: got there, they put me on Jim, the horse

speaker: BC
words: Bob.

speaker: IA
words: What? What was he?

speaker: BC
words: Bob. Called the horse Bob.

speaker: IA
words: Bob, because of his bobbed ears and his bobbed tail. And Mc Peek said,

speaker: IA
words: "You ride Bob because he is so surefooted." And, he said, "You ride

speaker: IA
words: out on Bob." Well, I had taken cold a little while before and I was

speaker: IA
words: subject to attacks of quinsy, and so they dolled me up in everything

speaker: IA
words: they could find in the cabin that was warm and I mounted Bob and we

speaker: IA
words: started out. Well, there was one place that we used to call Little

speaker: IA
words: Lake Thelma, it's a different name than that now on the map, and I

speaker: IA
words: don't know what it's called now. But at any rate, you had to go up a

speaker: IA
words: very high, very steep ridge to get up to the top and come on over into

speaker: IA
words: the Clarkia country, and we always in going up hung onto our horse's

speaker: IA
words: tail and made the horse pull us up instead of riding because it was

speaker: IA
words: this way, it was so rough you couldn't ride comfortably on it and we

speaker: IA
words: always held onto the horse's tail. Well, I was on Bob, which had neither

speaker: IA
words: ears nor tail, and so I had to stay on the horse and I'd been having

speaker: IA
words: the quinsy at the cabin and was not in a very good condition to be ri

speaker: IA
words: ding out there anyway, so we got to the- what was called the- not the

speaker: IA
words: company, it was the halfway cabin, it was a government cabin, and they

speaker: IA
words: had blankets and cooking utensils and heating and so forth at the cabin.

speaker: IA
words: We got to the cabin that night and who was at the cabin but Elsie

speaker: IA
words: Pfiel. She had heard that-

speaker: BC
words: Ione's giving a recording, can you come again?

speaker: SS
words: So you met- So Elsie Pfiel was there?

speaker: IA
words: Elsie Pfiel had heard through the- well, I suppose it would be the wireless (Chuckles)

speaker: IA
words: that Mc Peek was coming out to our place and she wan

speaker: IA
words: ted to come out to Clarkia, so she walked from her place and down to

speaker: IA
words: this government cabin, because she knew we'd stop there, and she had

speaker: IA
words: with her a great, big, bulldog, and that was for protection on her ca

speaker: IA
words: bin that she had this big bulldog. Well, she was at the cabin that

speaker: IA
words: night when we got in. And I had this enormous head of hair and that

speaker: IA
words: rain had wet the neck of the turtleneckI was wearing, and the sweater

speaker: IA
words: was wet and we couldn't get out of the sweater. So Mc Peek took one

speaker: IA
words: -top side, the one side; Elsie took the other and they took my hair

speaker: IA
words: down and just skinned this turtleneck sweater up over my head until

speaker: IA
words: they finally got it off and I was made as comfortable as possible. El

speaker: IA
words: sie and I slept in one bed that night, those little bunkbeds in the

speaker: IA
words: government cabin and along in the night came the pack rats. And the

speaker: IA
words: bulldog didn't like pack rats, so he started chasing them all around

speaker: IA
words: the house, in the cabin, all around the floor and whenever the pack

speaker: IA
words: rat - the dog would get too close, the pack rat ended up on the bed

speaker: IA
words: and the dog up after him! You can imagine how much sleep went on in

speaker: IA
words: that cabin that night! We couldn't calm the dog down on account of

speaker: IA
words: the rat and we couldn't catch the rat'and he couldn't catch the rat!

speaker: SS
words: Couldn't just kick him out of the cabin?

speaker: IA
words: Couldn't chase him out. They didn't have any idea of going out of the

speaker: IA
words: cabin.

speaker: SS
words: The dog.

speaker: IA
words: The dog?

speaker: BC
words: Yeah.

speaker: IA
words: Oh, no, it was cold. Snow deep outside. You don't put a dog out in

speaker: IA
words: the snow and leave him, especially a short haired dog like a bull

speaker: IA
words: dog. But we got up the next morning and went on into Clarkia and

speaker: IA
words: Elsie took the dog and went with us, and we all got to Clarkia safe!

speaker: IA
words: And that was quite a trip that time.

speaker: SS
words: Your father had come out all that distance by snowshoe? He snowshoed

speaker: SS
words: out to the cabin?

speaker: IA
words: Yes. Snowshoes or walking.

speaker: BC
words: Which because he person.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when you got to Clarkia you still had a long way to go to Wallace.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get on a train then?

speaker: IA
words: No. Oh, I had a long ways to go. Yes, my father stayed there and with

speaker: IA
words: me for two or three days til I got my throat cleared out, and then we

speaker: IA
words: went on up to Wallace and got there in time for the trial. The diffi

speaker: IA
words: culty was that the railroad had placed- what do you say? entries? Had

speaker: IA
words: filed on a lot of what was supposed to be railroadlland in that section

speaker: IA
words: of the country, and they were given by the government certain townships

speaker: IA
words: and certain- oh, what do you call it?- They had their rights- the rail

speaker: IA
words: roads had rights over certain townships in order to settle up this part

speaker: IA
words: of the country. And some of those railroads- townships- or filings

speaker: IA
words: were over on my place and part of them were on Mrs. Calkin's place.

speaker: IA
words: So we all had to go in for the government hearing at Wallace. But we

speaker: IA
words: could take the train from Teko and make connections and get up to Wal

speaker: IA
words: lace for the trial.

speaker: SS
words: Did you win that time?

speaker: IA
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: You lost that?

speaker: IA
words: I lost it. I lived there for twenty-two months or more and fought the

speaker: IA
words: fire and fed the firemen with the help and made the trips back and

speaker: IA
words: forth. But I lost it.VkDid the best we could and Burton L. French

speaker: IA
words: was the representative from Idaho back at Washington, D. C, and

speaker: IA
words: Burton tried his best to have it reopened and a new man put on, but

speaker: IA
words: they said no, that it wasn't, that I was over on the other- my location

speaker: IA
words: had gone over; that I hadn't lived out there a sufficient length of

speaker: IA
words: time. They sent out one of these little- we called them patent leather

speaker: IA
words: men- from Washington, D. C. Patent leather shoe men, that didn't know

speaker: IA
words: a darn thing about timber or living in the woods or even living! And

speaker: IA
words: he'd come in and he'd look over your little cabin and say, "It's not a

speaker: IA
words: suitable habitation." And I had a little garden patch, and I'd raised

speaker: IA
words: radishes and lettuce out there and all. He looked at my garden patch

speaker: IA
words: and says, "It's not sufficient cultivation."

speaker: SS
words: To your face?

speaker: IA
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: To your face?

speaker: IA
words: Yes, oh, yes. He didn't mind telling us. That went on like that, he

speaker: IA
words: came in and he got Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Torson's place and my place

speaker: IA
words: and Kit Calkins place right next to mine where she was located on one

speaker: IA
words: end of the meadow and I was at the other end of the meadow. Our cabins

speaker: IA
words: were quite close, so that I had company out there. And she lost hers,

speaker: IA
words: and I don't know, there was a whole bunch of them in through there that

speaker: IA
words: lost it, on account of this man from Washington, D. C. that didn't think

speaker: IA
words: people could live under those conditions, although we were living under

speaker: IA
words: them. Well, during the fire season in 1910, this same man was put in

speaker: IA
words: charge of the firefighters brigade, that was sent out into that part of

speaker: IA
words: the country. And at that time, he had sent his report in to Washing

speaker: IA
words: ton, D. C. and they'd cancelled all of our homesteads out there, but we

speaker: IA
words: were still on the homesteads, because we didn't know but what it might

speaker: IA
words: be opened- or reopened, at any time. You never know when you were figh

speaker: IA
words: ting it. And he was put on in charge of the firefighting brigade that

speaker: IA
words: went out into that section of the country. And one morning Mrs. Taylor

speaker: IA
words: SIDE B

speaker: IA
words: who lived alone about a mile and a half or two miles from Mi rs. Calkins

speaker: IA
words: place, and she was a little uneasy about the smoke that had been drif

speaker: IA
words: ting into the country, so she came over to stay with me. So as not to

speaker: IA
words: be alone at her cabin in case anything should happen. Well, we werethere

speaker: IA
words: was a rap at the door, as it was just barely daylight outside,

speaker: IA
words: and Mrs. Taylor got up and answered the door, and there was this man

speaker: IA
words: who had reported all these homesteaders, standing at the door and he

speaker: IA
words: said that he was in charge of a fire fighting crew and that they were

speaker: IA
words: trying to locate the fire which was causing all this smoke in this part

speaker: IA
words: of the country. And he wanted to know if there was a trail any farther

speaker: IA
words: east. So she told him of her trail that went farther east and he said

speaker: IA
words: he'd take his crew and go over there. Well, we got up and dressed and

speaker: IA
words: decided that we'd walk over and see if. they had found her cabin and if

speaker: IA
words: they were still there; which they were when we got there. And the men

speaker: IA
words: that were camping around the fire on the outside, some of them, and

speaker: IA
words: some of them were inside of the cabin, and were trying to get something

speaker: IA
words: for breakfast. And Mrs. Taylor said that she would help the men. So

speaker: IA
words: she got busy and fixed pancackes and things for the men inside t he

speaker: IA
words: cabin and outside the cabin. And when they went to move on then to lo

speaker: IA
words: cate farther to locate the fire, Mr, Roach said to Mrs. Taylor, "Woul

speaker: IA
words: dn't you ladies like to go on with us a little farther and get a meal

speaker: IA
words: for us at noon? So we'd have something to eat." And we said we would.

speaker: IA
words: We got them a lunch of pancackes or rolled cakes so they'd

speaker: IA
words: have something to eat at noon, and that evening then, they wanted us to

speaker: IA
words: stay and get the evening meal for them. Well, that was my first attempt

speaker: IA
words: atCooking outside over a fireplace, and also Mrs. Taylor's first attempt,

speaker: IA
words: But we managed to get something for the men to eat that night and they

speaker: IA
words: fixed a bed for us right along by the side of the horses that

speaker: IA
words: they had taken in there. And the horses kept stomping and chewing and

speaker: IA
words: stomping and trying to fight the flies all night long, so we didn't

speaker: IA
words: get very much sleep. But the next morning they wanted us to continue

speaker: IA
words: on and take care of the men we had.

speaker: BC
words: How many did you have?

speaker: IA
words: We had about ten or fifteen that we were trying to fix for them, and

speaker: IA
words: they had their own provisions, but we had to take care of the provisions

speaker: IA
words: and the cooking. By the way, it took me years to like potatoes! I

speaker: IA
words: cooked so many potatoes in waterbuckets over the fire, that I didn't

speaker: IA
words: like potatoes for a long, long time.

speaker: SS
words: Were you prepared to go on with them? You didn't have your clothing

speaker: SS
words: and all your belongings, did you?

speaker: IA
words: No, we just wore what we had and got along for the time being. Because

speaker: BC
words: A little knapsack.) they had to keep on working and going farther. They had a

speaker: BC
words: man what

speaker: BC
words: was his name? Johnny, Johnny, Johnny- at any rate, he was a man here

speaker: BC
words: in town that we knew of that was a cook and he had gone on a fire figh

speaker: BC
words: ting crew just for the experience and was a cook. And they left him

speaker: BC
words: up on the top of what was called the Freezeout Ridge when they camped

speaker: BC
words: there the night before coming on to our cabin, and they left him up

speaker: BC
words: there and he said that he would pack things and have «Nieything all ready

speaker: BC
